title: Screen time, problematic media use, and clinical concerns in the ABCD Study: Differences by sex and race/ethnicity.
abstract: This study assesses the relation between screen time, problematic media use behaviors, and clinical concerns (internalizing and externalizing problems) and suicidal ideation and non-suicidal self-injury within race/ethnicity and sex in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (youth aged 11 to 12; N = 10,052). Understanding behaviors around screens (problematic media use), rather than focusing on screen time alone is useful in guiding clinical recommendations. In this analysis, regression models indicated that problematic media use consistently predicted clinical concerns with a larger effect size than screen media use. When examining how problematic media use and screen media use related to clinical concerns along domains of race/ethnicity and sex, problematic media use was a more consistent predictor of clinical concerns than screen media use for almost every race/ethnicity (except American Indian/Alaska Native participants). Problematic media use was also a consistent predictor of clinical concerns for both males and females, with some difference in screen media use predictors. This study has implications for the utility of assessing screen media use in research on clinical concerns in youth, and further suggests that researchers and clinicians should consider behaviors around screens in addition to screen time itself when assessing for impact on mental health.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data (Youth aged 11 to 12; N=10,052); explicitly states data from 'Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (youth aged 11 to 12; N = 10,052)'

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures screen time (parent-report and youth-report in hours per day) and problematic media use (Problematic Media Use Measure - Short Form with parent-reported items assessing problematic screen media behaviors)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multilevel regression models with random effects predicting clinical concerns (CBCL, BPM, SI, NSSI) from screen media use and problematic media use. Models include fixed effects for both predictors.

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports standardized beta coefficients with SEs: e.g., problematic media use predicting CBCL total β=0.42, screen media use β=0.01 for internalizing problems; Table 2 provides multiple coefficients across outcome variables

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical multilevel regression analysis conducted on ABCD Study cross-sectional data from Year 2 time point

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Development and Psychopathology, 2025

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Screen time and problematic media use are independent variables predicting clinical outcome variables (internalizing/externalizing problems, suicidal ideation). Analysis includes individual psychological/clinical outcomes (not demographic-only predictors).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data, 4.0 data release, Year 2 assessment; N=10,052 youth aged 11-12 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures 'screen time' and 'problematic media use' but neither meets criteria. Screen time is generic total screen time (not smartphone/SM-specific). Problematic media use is a composite scale (PMPU-SF) but does not specify smartphone or social media platform. Per FT-IC2: 'Generic total screen time or STS composite with no phone/SM breakdown' = NOT_MET

FT_IC3.evidence: Multilevel regression models predicting clinical concerns (CBCL, BPM, SI, NSSI); unstandardized and standardized coefficients reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports unstandardized β coefficients and standard errors. Example from Table 2: Problematic media use predicting CBCL total β=7.73*** [0.21], internalizing β=1.74*** [0.07], externalizing β=2.50*** [0.07]

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis using cross-sectional multilevel regression models examining associations between screen behaviors and clinical concerns

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Development and Psychopathology, 2025

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Fails FT-IC2 (not a specific smartphone/SM variable). EC7 cannot apply when FT-IC2 is NOT_MET. Additionally, even if screen time/problematic media use were coded as MET, the paper includes cross-sectional analyses with non-SM outcomes (clinical concerns), which prevents EC7 from applying

title: Longitudinal relationships between lifestyle risk factors and neurodevelopment in early adolescence.
journal: Health psychology : official journal of the Division of Health Psychology, American Psychological Association
abstract: OBJECTIVE: The goal of this study is to investigate the cross-sectional and longitudinal relationships between clustered lifestyle risk factors (sleep, physical activity, body mass index [BMI], and screen time) and neurodevelopment over the early adolescent period. METHOD: Data from the ABCD Study Data Release 3.0 consisted of 11,878 participants (aged 9-10 years) at baseline and 6,571 participants (aged 11-12 years) at 2-year follow-up. The interrelationships between lifestyle risk factors and brain structure were analyzed using bivariate multiple indicator latent change score models. Using confirmatory factor analysis, a single lifestyle risk factor domain (measured by sleep, physical activity, BMI, and screen time) was shown to fit the data well. Using exploratory and confirmatory factor analysis, seven brain structure domains were extracted and labeled as temporal-parietal, frontotemporal, occipital, orbitofrontal, temporal, cingulate, parietal, and cuneus domains. All bivariate latent change score models accounted for age, sex at birth, race/ethnicity, parental education, and marital status. RESULTS: Higher lifestyle risk was associated with smaller brain volume at baseline. Higher baseline lifestyle risk was also associated with a greater rate of change (i.e., greater decreases) in brain volume for the temporal-parietal, frontotemporal, orbitofrontal, parietal, and cuneus domains. Effects were not reciprocal; baseline brain volume did not predict changes in lifestyle behaviors over time. CONCLUSION: These findings are important for understanding the biological mechanisms underpinning health risk factors and can be used to target interventions and improve brain health during this critical developmental phase. (PsycInfo Database Record (c) 2023 APA, all rights reserved).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Data Release 3.0 with 11,878 participants at baseline aged 9-10 years and 6,571 at 2-year follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time is measured but only as part of a composite latent variable (lifestyle risk factor domain) alongside sleep, physical activity, and BMI. No separately extractable coefficient for screen time as its own variable; only the aggregated domain coefficient is reported.

FT_IC3.evidence: Screen time is not separately analyzed as an independent exposure. Bivariate latent change score models test the lifestyle risk factor DOMAIN (composite of sleep, PA, BMI, screen time) vs brain volume, not screen time alone.

FT_IC4.evidence: No numeric coefficient reported specifically for screen time (β, OR, r, p) as a standalone variable. Only domain-level statistics available.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis. Longitudinal design with bivariate multiple indicator latent change score models examining relationships between lifestyle risk factors and brain structure from baseline to 2-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Health Psychology, 2023. Not a preprint, duplicate, or protocol-only paper.

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Lifestyle risk factor domain is the predictor (not DV), and brain volume is the outcome. Paper reports both cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses. This is not FT-EC7 scenario (which applies when phone/SM is DV with only demographic predictors in longitudinal context).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Data Release 3.0 with 11,878 participants aged 9-10 years at baseline and 6,571 at 2-year follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures recreational screen time via Youth Screen Time Survey (Bagot et al., 2018), reported as average hours per weekday/weekend and weekly screen time

FT_IC3.evidence: Bivariate multiple indicator latent change score models with inferential tests linking lifestyle risk factor domain (including screen time) to brain volume domains (correlation Φ, path γ2). Multiple statistically significant pathways reported (e.g., temporal-parietal: β = −0.134, p = .002)

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple β coefficients, z-scores, and p-values reported in Table 2. Example: temporal-parietal baseline covariance β = −0.197 (0.026), Z = −7.661, p < .001

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using longitudinal latent change score modeling on cross-sectional and 2-year follow-up ABCD data

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article, Health Psychology, 2023. Not identified as duplicate

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is one component of a clustered lifestyle risk factor domain (also includes sleep, physical activity, BMI). Analyses include cross-sectional associations and screen time as a predictor of brain volume changes (not as a dependent variable only). Therefore FT-EC7 does not apply.

title: One-year predictions of delayed reward discounting in the adolescent brain cognitive development study.
abstract: Delayed reward discounting (DRD) refers to the extent to which an individual devalues a reward based on a temporal delay and is known to be elevated in individuals with substance use disorders and many mental illnesses. DRD has been linked previously with both features of brain structure and function, as well as various behavioral, psychological, and life-history factors. However, there has been little work on the neurobiological and behavioral antecedents of DRD in childhood. This is an important question, as understanding the antecedents of DRD can provide signs of mechanisms in the development of psychopathology. The present study used baseline data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (N = 4,042) to build machine learning models to predict DRD at the first follow-up visit, 1 year later. In separate machine learning models, we tested elastic net regression, random forest regression, light gradient boosting regression, and support vector regression. In five-fold cross-validation on the training set, models using an array of questionnaire/task variables were able to predict DRD, with these findings generalizing to a held-out (i.e., "lockbox") test set of 20% of the sample. Key predictive variables were neuropsychological test performance at baseline, socioeconomic status, screen media activity, psychopathology, parenting, and personality. However, models using magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)-derived brain variables did not reliably predict DRD in either the cross-validation or held-out test set. These results suggest a combination of questionnaire/task variables as antecedents of excessive DRD in late childhood, which may presage the development of problematic substance use in adolescence. (PsycInfo Database Record (c) 2022 APA, all rights reserved).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study baseline data, N=4042 with follow-up at 1 year; assessed at ages 9-10 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Includes 'screen media activity' as one of 131 questionnaire/task predictor variables in machine learning models. Measures typical weekly screen time on computers, cellphones, tablets, and electronic devices (watching TV, videos, gaming, texting, social media, video chatting).

FT_IC3.evidence: Machine learning predictive models with cross-validation test screen media activity and other variables as predictors of delayed reward discounting (DRD) at 1-year follow-up. Models tested multiple algorithms (elastic net, random forest, light gradient boosting, support vector regression).

FT_IC4.evidence: Key predictive variables identified: 'screen media activity' listed among top predictive variables (neuropsychological test performance, socioeconomic status, screen media activity, psychopathology, parenting, personality). ML coefficients reported via model performance metrics.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: Machine learning predictive models with cross-validation and held-out test set, predicting DRD from baseline variables

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Experimental and Clinical Psychopharmacology, 2022); no evidence of duplicate

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Does NOT apply. Screen media activity is an INDEPENDENT variable (predictor) in the ML models, not a dependent variable. Outcome is delayed reward discounting (DRD), a non-SM variable. Paper includes individual psychological/clinical predictor (DRD as a behavioral/clinical outcome). Does not meet FT-EC7 criteria (which requires phone/SM as DV with only demographic predictors).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, baseline N=4,042 (from ABCD data release 2.0.1 with 11,875 participants at baseline)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen media activity is listed as a KEY PREDICTIVE VARIABLE in the abstract/results but is NOT the exposure/outcome under primary investigation. The PRIMARY outcome is Delayed Reward Discounting (DRD), a cognitive decision-making measure. Screen time serves as a COVARIATE/PREDICTOR in machine learning models predicting DRD. Fail: smartphone/SM is neither IV nor DV in the hypothesized association of interest.

FT_IC3.evidence: Not applicable: FT_IC2 not met. No inferential test specifically linking smartphone/SM variable to a non-SM outcome.

FT_IC4.evidence: Not applicable: FT_IC2 not met. Paper does not report extractable coefficients for SM↔outcome associations; screen media only mentioned as contributing feature in ML feature importance, without specific numerical values.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis; prospective machine learning study predicting one-year-follow-up DRD from baseline measures

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Experimental and Clinical Psychopharmacology, 2022

C2.FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 not applicable: EXCLUDED at FT_IC2 criterion. Screen media is neither the primary exposure nor the primary outcome; it appears only as one predictor among many in ML models predicting DRD (a cognitive outcome, not phone/SM use).
